column1: # Each row is a full Bash command string. The verdict is checked against the
# ACTUAL parser output: the row is run through parser.ParseBash and the
# resulting parsed.commands list is fed to #command{rm}.
# match   = parsed.commands contains "rm" (the resolved executable name)
# A literal TAB in the input column is written as the two chars \t and
# Different command whose name starts with rm but is not rm.
# Escalation/env/whitespace prefixes: under #command{rm} the matcher reads
# parsed.commands, so the escalation prefix is stripped (sudo) and the leading
# whitespace / assignment prefix no longer hides rm — these now MATCH. This is
# the R3 semantic reversal vs. the old ^rm\b raw-string matcher.
# `env rm` resolves to commands=["env"] (env is not an escalation prefix; it
# becomes the leading command), and `echo rm` resolves to commands=["echo"]:
# rm is an argument, not the executable. Both stay nomatch.
